claim: The IPBES Assessment Report found that 16 percent of vertebrate pollinators are threatened with extinction, wild pollinator populations are declining in many regions, and pollinator-dependent crops contribute 5-8 percent of global crop production by volume, valued at $235-577 billion annually.

source.title: IPBES Assessment Report on Pollinators, Pollination and Food Production

method: Reviewed IPBES report chapters on pollinator status, drivers of decline, crop-pollination dependence, and geographic evidence gaps. Focused on the documented concentration of studies in Europe and North America.
limitations: The 16 percent figure is for vertebrate pollinators only — insect pollinator status is far less documented.
Over 80 percent of studies in the assessment come from Europe and North America — LMIC pollinator trends are largely inferred, not observed.
The $235-577 billion valuation range depends on crop-production price assumptions and pollination-dependence classifications that vary by methodology.
The assessment is from 2016 — pollinator decline has accelerated in some regions and new monitoring data has emerged in others.

claim: MapSPAM provides global crop-production maps at approximately 10km resolution distinguishing pollinator-dependent crops (fruits, vegetables, nuts, oilseeds) from wind-pollinated staples (rice, wheat, maize), enabling sub-national estimation of pollination-service dependence.
method: Reviewed MapSPAM documentation for crop classification methodology, spatial resolution, geographic coverage, and pollination-dependence categorization approach.
limitations: MapSPAM crop-production maps are modeled allocations based on subnational statistics, not direct field observations — they have allocation uncertainty particularly in mixed-cropping systems.
Pollination-dependence classifications are based on global averages from primarily temperate-zone studies — tropical crop varieties may have different pollination requirements.
The 10km resolution smooths fine-scale crop diversity in smallholder mosaic landscapes where pollinator habitat and crop fields are intermixed.
MapSPAM does not distinguish between irrigated and rainfed production, which affects both crop-pollinator interactions and yield vulnerability.

claim: Satellite-derived land-cover and land-use change data at 10-30m resolution from Sentinel-2 and Landsat can track habitat fragmentation, agricultural intensification, and landscape simplification — providing indirect indicators of pollinator habitat pressure in the absence of direct monitoring.
method: Reviewed Sentinel-2 and Landsat land-cover classification products for spatial resolution, temporal coverage, habitat-class detail, and validation studies in agricultural landscapes.
limitations: Land-cover classification accuracy varies by region — tropical and dryland agricultural mosaics have lower classification accuracy than temperate cropland.

Habitat fragmentation metrics derived from land-cover maps are proxies for pollinator habitat quality — they do not measure floral resource availability, nesting site availability, or pesticide exposure.

The relationship between landscape simplification and pollinator abundance is documented primarily from European and North American studies — transferability to tropical agricultural landscapes is uncertain.
Temporal resolution of land-cover maps (annual or multi-year composites) may miss seasonal habitat changes important for pollinator life cycles.
